local_names.romanized_local: Zucchini e Gamberetti (Italian: zucchini and prawns)

aliases: Prawns with Zucchini and Cherry Tomato

english: Prawns with Zucchini and Cherry Tomato

food_subgroup: Seafood dish with vegetables and olive oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for zucchini e gamberetti; closest parent 'shrimp dish' (FOODON_03301116) used as anchor. Zucchini and cherry tomato components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent for this prawn-dominant dish. No composite Italian seafood-vegetable dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Italian prawn and zucchini preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of zucchini e gamberetti is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of sautéed prawns, sliced zucchini, and cherry tomatoes dressed with olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180) and Zucchini, cooked (FDC ID 169291) and Tomatoes, cherry, raw (FDC ID 170457) and Olive oil (FDC ID 171413); composite estimate weighted ~55% prawn, 30% zucchini, 10% cherry tomato, 5% olive oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Italian dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries: cooked shrimp (~55% by weight), cooked zucchini (~30%), cherry tomatoes (~10%), olive oil (~5%). Weighted composite per-100g values calculated from these proportions. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: boiled/cooked shrimp and non-starchy vegetables (zucchini, tomato) composite; GI estimated at 15 based on very low carbohydrate content and low-GI vegetable components.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for zucchini e gamberetti. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008): cooked shrimp has negligible carbohydrate and effectively zero GI; zucchini and cherry tomatoes are non-starchy low-GI vegetables (GI ~15). Olive oil has no GI contribution. Composite GI of approximately 15 is defensible. GL per 300 g serving = (15 × 3.1 × 300/100) / 100 = 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from prawns and cooking
hypertension.guidance: Zucchini e gamberetti contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (930 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a significant portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 15, zucchini e gamberetti has a glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added starch or sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil monounsaturated fats and lean protein from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil (approximately 2.4 g per 100 g) and lean protein from prawns with low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, though the cholesterol content (~130 mg per 100 g from prawns) should be noted for individuals with strict dietary cholesterol targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zucchini e Gamberetti is a classic Italian seafood dish consisting of prawns (gamberetti) sautéed with sliced zucchini (courgette), cherry tomatoes, and olive oil. The dish is a staple of Italian coastal and trattoria cuisine, celebrated for its simplicity, freshness, and light Mediterranean character. Prawns are typically pan-cooked until just pink and tender, while zucchini is lightly sautéed to retain a slight bite, and cherry tomatoes add a burst of acidity and sweetness. Olive oil serves as both the cooking medium and a flavour component, contributing monounsaturated fats characteristic of the Mediterranean diet. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for a range of dietary requirements. Nutritionally, it is high in lean protein (~13 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~3 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (~4 g per 100 g), with the fat profile dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15), making it an excellent option for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the natural sodium in prawns and any seasoning, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is rich in vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the prawns, and provides vitamin C and vitamin A from the vegetables. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and represents a light, protein-forward main course suitable for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Italian dish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked shrimp, cooked zucchini, cherry tomatoes, and olive oil using a weighted composite approach. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' as the dish is prawn-dominant with no grain component. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central (cooked shrimp FDC 175180, cooked zucchini FDC 169291, cherry tomatoes FDC 170457, olive oil FDC 171413) weighted approximately 55:30:10:5 by mass. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients of this dish (cooked shrimp FDC 175180, cooked zucchini FDC 169291, cherry tomatoes FDC 170457, olive oil FDC 171413); a defensible composite estimate cannot be made from the available source data. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed effectively zero for this combination of natural seafood, vegetables, and olive oil per USDA FoodData Central component data. 5. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy vegetable and cooked shrimp data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 6. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this dish is served in Singapore context; Italian is the origin language and the food name itself is Italian. 7. Ontology anchors use closest_parent matches as no exact composite dish codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2 for this preparation. 8. Restaurant source: Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data). Price SGD 29.32.
